text:
*[Bug 19049] New: btpe implementation rbinom has incorrect signs for error terms in Stirling approximation*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19049>

            Bug ID: 19049
           Summary: btpe implementation rbinom has incorrect signs for
                    error terms in Stirling approximation
           Product: R
           Version: R 4.6.x
          Hardware: Other
                OS: Other
            Status: UNCONFIRMED
          Severity: normal
          Priority: P5
         Component: Language
          Assignee: <mailto:R-core@R-project.org|R-core@R-project.org>
          Reporter: <mailto:k88hudson@gmail.com|k88hudson@gmail.com>

Created attachment 3671
  --> <https://bugs.r-project.org/attachment.cgi?id=3671&action=edit>
Fix for error terms in BTPE Stirling approximation

I have been looking at implementations of the BTPE algorithm across several
languages and it seems that R's implementation in rbinom inherits an
error in the 1988 paper by Kachitvichyanukul and Schmeiser [1] from which
the algorithm originates. The signs for the error terms in the Stirling
approximation in step 5.3 are incorrect; all four terms are added,
but the last two should be subtracted.

Step 5.3 (Page 219):

Note that the formula shifts by 1 to handle 0:
```
# ln(n!) = n*ln(n+1) - (n+1) + log(2*pi*(n+1))/2 + stirling_correction(n+1) +
O((n+1)^-11)
stirling_correction = function(x) {
  1/(12*x) - 1/(360*x^3) + 1/(1260*x^5) - 1/(1680*x^7) + 1/(1188*x^9)
}
xM = M + 0.5
x1 = y + 1
f1 = M + 1
z = n + 1 - M
w = n - y + 1
```

In the paper:
```
xM * log(f1/x1) +
  (n - M + 0.5) * log(z/w) +
  (y - M) * log(w*r/(x1*q)) +
  stirling_correction(f1) +
  stirling_correction(z) +
  stirling_correction(x1) +
  stirling_correction(w)
```

The correction should be:
```
xM * log(f1/x1) +
  (n - M + 0.5) * log(z/w) +
  (y - M) * log(w*r/(x1*q)) +
  stirling_correction(f1) +
  stirling_correction(z) -
  stirling_correction(x1) -
  stirling_correction(w)
```

You can see the signs should be different in that the first two error terms
stem from the approximation to the numerator and the second from the
denominator of equation (1) on page 218, and hence have different signs.

As far as I know, the authors of GSL[2] were first to notice and fix it. My
collaborator Jason Asher (@jasonasher) worked through the derivation of
the equation and we independently confirm the error.

I've included a patch. Let me know if you would like
more details or there is something I should do in terms of contributing the
fix.


[1] <https://doi.org/10.1145/42372.42381>
[2]
<https://github.com/ampl/gsl/blob/fb0cfbc82b5e9e4d22444efce9241924f4aefb83/randist/binomial_tpe.c#L339-L361>

Fix for error terms in BTPE Stirling approximation

I have been looking at implementations of the BTPE algorithm across several
languages and it seems that R's implementation in rbinom inherits an
error in the 1988 paper by Kachitvichyanukul and Schmeiser [1] from which
the algorithm originates. The signs for the error terms in the Stirling
approximation in step 5.3 are incorrect; all four terms are added,
but the last two should be subtracted.

Step 5.3 (Page 219):

Note that the formula shifts by 1 to handle 0:

xM * log(f1/x1) +
  (n - M + 0.5) * log(z/w) +
  (y - M) * log(w*r/(x1*q)) +
  stirling_correction(f1) +
  stirling_correction(z) +
  stirling_correction(x1) +
  stirling_correction(w)

xM * log(f1/x1) +
  (n - M + 0.5) * log(z/w) +
  (y - M) * log(w*r/(x1*q)) +
  stirling_correction(f1) +
  stirling_correction(z) -
  stirling_correction(x1) -
  stirling_correction(w)

You can see the signs should be different in that the first two error terms
stem from the approximation to the numerator and the second from the
denominator of equation (1) on page 218, and hence have different signs.

As far as I know, the authors of GSL[2] were first to notice and fix it. My
collaborator Jason Asher (@jasonasher) worked through the derivation of
the equation and we independently confirm the error.

I've included a patch. Let me know if you would like
more details or there is something I should do in terms of contributing the
fix.
